skill.name: ai-video-generation
skill.description: Generate AI video prompts and run inference.sh belt CLI examples for text-to-video, image-to-video, avatar, lipsync, editing, upscaling, foley, and media merging workflows.
skill.summary: Create AI video workflows using inference.sh belt commands for 40+ supported video models.
skill.author: skills-shell

security_audit.summary: Static command findings are mostly false positives from Markdown backticks and fenced shell examples, not Ruby execution. The skill still has real medium risk because it grants Bash access to the belt CLI and sends prompts or media URLs to inference.sh model services. The weak cryptography findings at SKILL.md:3 and SKILL.md:75 have no supporting evidence in context.

medium_findings.title: Networked CLI Execution for AI Video Jobs
medium_findings.description: The skill allows Bash access for belt commands and provides many belt app run examples. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it can execute remote model jobs and transmit prompts or media URLs to external services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and repeated belt app run examples confirm external command execution. The behavior matches the skill purpose, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: External Media URL Processing
medium_findings.description: Examples pass image, audio, and video URLs to remote AI services. Users could unintentionally process private media or sensitive prompts through third-party infrastructure.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple examples include image_url, audio_url, video_url, and reference_image fields. No exfiltration intent is present, but remote processing of user media is clear.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Command Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported Ruby or shell backtick execution across Markdown tables and code examples. In context these are documentation backticks and fenced shell snippets, not executable Ruby code inside the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited areas are Markdown inline code, tables, and examples. There is no Ruby file and no evidence of dynamic backtick evaluation.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Unsupported
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged weak cryptography at the description and utility table header lines. No cryptographic API, algorithm selection, hashing, or encryption behavior is present at those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is frontmatter description text and line 75 is a Markdown table header. The reviewed file contains no cryptographic implementation evidence.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation and Example Inputs
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to documentation links, an image link, and placeholder media URL examples. They are not hidden endpoints or credential exfiltration targets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible in documentation and examples, and the service domain matches the stated inference.sh purpose. Placeholder media URLs still imply network use, but not malicious routing.

dangerous_patterns.title: Permitted Bash Wrapper for belt CLI
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill enables Bash commands matching belt, which can start remote jobs through the inference.sh CLI. This should be disclosed because it can consume credits and process user media remotely.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The tool permission and quick start commands directly show belt command execution. The pattern is expected for this skill but requires user awareness.

content.value_statement: Creating AI videos across many providers requires model selection and exact CLI inputs. This skill gives ready workflows for belt-based video generation, animation, editing, lipsync, upscaling, and sound effects.
actual_capabilities: Lists video model options for text-to-video, image-to-video, avatar, lipsync, editing, and utility tasks.
Provides belt CLI commands for running inference.sh video apps.
Shows input examples for prompts, image URLs, audio URLs, video URLs, duration, resolution, and audio generation.
Documents related skills for specialized video, speech, image, and publishing workflows.
Links to inference.sh documentation for running apps, streaming results, and content pipelines.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh belt CLI and a working login before commands can run.
Generated output quality, duration, and resolution depend on the selected external model.
Media URLs and prompts may be sent to external services for processing.
The skill documents workflows but does not include local video generation code.

use_cases.title: Create social media clips
use_cases.description: Generate short video concepts from text prompts or animate existing images for campaign assets.

use_cases.title: Prototype product demo footage
use_cases.description: Use text-to-video and video editing examples to test visual directions before production work.

use_cases.title: Build avatar and lipsync assets
use_cases.description: Run avatar and lipsync workflows with portrait and audio inputs for explainer or training content.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a basic video
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a short AI video from this idea: [describe scene]. Use a fast model and explain the belt command before running it.

prompt_templates.title: Animate an image
prompt_templates.prompt: Animate this image URL: [image URL]. Add gentle motion based on this direction: [motion notes]. Choose an appropriate image-to-video model.

prompt_templates.title: Create a talking avatar
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a talking head video using this portrait URL: [portrait URL] and this audio URL: [audio URL]. Pick a lipsync or avatar model.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a complete video pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a belt-based video workflow for [campaign goal]. Include model choices for generation, editing, upscaling, sound effects, and final merging.

output_examples.input: A request to make a five second launch teaser from a written product concept.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends a fast text-to-video model, drafts a concise prompt, and provides the belt command to start the job.

output_examples.input: A portrait image URL and narration audio URL for an explainer clip.
output_examples.output: The assistant selects an avatar or lipsync model, explains the required inputs, and prepares the command for remote processing.

output_examples.input: Two generated video clips that need sound effects and a transition.
output_examples.output: The assistant proposes a foley step and a media merge step, with the inputs needed for each belt run.

best_practices: Confirm that each prompt and media URL is safe to send to inference.sh before running commands.
Start with fast or economical models when testing prompt direction and timing.
Keep generated clips, source URLs, and model settings recorded for repeatable workflows.
anti_patterns: Do not process private portraits, voices, or unreleased media without permission.
Do not run costly high quality models before validating the prompt with a smaller test.
Do not assume every model supports the same duration, resolution, audio, or reference inputs.

faq.question: Does this skill generate video locally?
faq.answer: No. It provides belt CLI workflows that run video apps through inference.sh services.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Do I need an inference.sh account?
faq.answer: Yes. The quick start requires belt login before running video generation commands.

faq.question: Can it animate existing images?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes image-to-video examples using image URLs and motion prompts.

faq.question: Can it make talking head videos?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents avatar and lipsync workflows using portrait and audio URLs.

faq.question: What should I check before publishing this skill?
faq.answer: Warn users that belt commands call external services and can process prompts, media URLs, and account credits.
